name: Acne Vulgaris
description: A chronic inflammatory skin condition affecting the pilosebaceous unit, characterized by comedones, papules, pustules, nodules, and cysts. Most common in adolescents but can persist into adulthood. Driven by follicular hyperkeratinization, increased sebum production, Cutibacterium acnes proliferation, and inflammation.
symptoms: Comedones (blackheads and whiteheads)
Inflammatory papules and pustules
Nodules and cysts in severe cases
Post-inflammatory erythema and hyperpigmentation
Scarring in severe or untreated cases
Predilection for face, chest, back, and shoulders
triggers: Dairy consumption and high glycemic load diets
red_flags: Sudden onset severe acne in adulthood (possible androgen excess)
Acne with hirsutism and irregular menses (possible PCOS)
Isolated severe nodules without comedones (possible gram-negative folliculitis)
Acne fulminans with systemic symptoms
Significant psychological distress or depression

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Post-inflammatory erythema (PIE) more prominent; erythematous macules persist for months
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Both PIE and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH) common; PIH may dominate
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation is primary concern; acne often undertreated due to fear of PIH; higher risk of keloid scarring

pregnancy_considerations: Category C: Avoid topical retinoids (tretinoin, adapalene, tazarotene) and oral isotretinoin. Safe options include azelaic acid, topical erythromycin/clindamycin, benzoyl peroxide (limited absorption). Oral tetracyclines contraindicated.

name: Hormonal Acne
description: A subtype of acne vulgaris driven by hormonal fluctuations, particularly androgens, which stimulate sebaceous gland activity. Typically presents in adult women along the jawline, chin, and neck. Often cyclical, flaring before menstruation.
symptoms: Deep, painful papules and nodules along jawline, chin, and neck
Cyclical pattern tied to menstrual cycle
Often minimal comedones compared to inflammatory lesions
Adult-onset (typically after age 25)
Resistant to conventional topical treatments
May persist or worsen during perimenopause
triggers: Menstrual cycle fluctuations (luteal phase progesterone rise)
High androgen index progestins in hormonal contraception
red_flags: New-onset hormonal acne with signs of virilization (deepening voice, increased body hair)
Severe acne with menstrual irregularities or infertility (possible PCOS)
Rapid onset with systemic symptoms (possible adrenal or ovarian tumor)
Refractory to standard hormonal therapy

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Post-inflammatory erythema prominent along jawline
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Mixed PIE and PIH; hyperpigmentation can be significant along jawline and neck
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation is major concern; jawline and neck PIH can be persistent and challenging to treat

pregnancy_considerations: Spironolactone absolutely contraindicated. Oral contraceptives containing anti-androgenic progestins (drospirenone, cyproterone) must be stopped. Safe: azelaic acid, benzoyl peroxide, topical clindamycin.

name: Acne Mechanica
description: A form of acne triggered by friction, pressure, heat, or occlusion of the skin. Common in athletes (helmet, chin strap, shoulder pads), musicians, and individuals who wear tight clothing or masks. Results from mechanical irritation of the pilosebaceous unit.
symptoms: Monomorphic papules and pustules at sites of friction/pressure
Distribution corresponds to equipment or clothing contact
Common on forehead (helmets), chin (masks), back and shoulders (backpacks, pads)
May lack comedones initially
Often worse in hot, humid conditions
triggers: Friction and pressure from sports equipment (helmets, chin straps, shoulder pads)
Backpack straps and tight bra straps
Heat and humidity combined with occlusion
red_flags: Lesions not improving with removal of trigger
Development of nodules, cysts, or scarring
Signs of secondary bacterial infection
Persistent despite 4–6 weeks of trigger avoidance

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Post-inflammatory erythema prominent; friction-induced erythema may be pronounced
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Risk of post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation at friction sites
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation significant; friction sites on darker skin are prone to hyperpigmentation and keloid formation

pregnancy_considerations: Management focuses on behavioral modifications (loose clothing, moisture-wicking fabrics, barrier protection). Topical benzoyl peroxide and azelaic acid safe. Avoid topical retinoids.

name: Eczema / Atopic Dermatitis
description: A chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin condition characterized by pruritus, erythema, dry skin, and eczematous lesions. Associated with type 2 immune response, skin barrier dysfunction (filaggrin mutations), and atopic diathesis (asthma, allergic rhinitis). Affects up to 20% of children and 3–7% of adults.
symptoms: Intense pruritus (itch-scratch cycle)
Erythematous, poorly demarcated patches and plaques
Dry, xerotic skin
Lichenification in chronic lesions
Excoriations and crusting
Oozing and weeping in acute flares
Infantile pattern: face, scalp, extensor surfaces; Adult pattern: flexural (antecubital, popliteal fossae), hands, feet
triggers: Environmental allergens (dust mites, pollen, pet dander)
Food allergens (eggs, milk, peanuts, soy, wheat — especially in children)
Skin infections (S. aureus colonization, herpes simplex)
red_flags: Eczema herpeticum: sudden eruption of painful, monomorphic vesicles with fever and malaise — EMERGENCY
Widespread bacterial infection with honey-colored crusting and systemic symptoms
Sudden severe flare unresponsive to usual management
Significant sleep disturbance and psychological distress
Growth failure in children with severe disease

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Erythema readily visible; post-inflammatory erythema common; photosensitivity may occur with phototherapy
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Erythema may appear violaceous or gray; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and hypopigmentation both common; lichenification may be subtle
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Erythema difficult to assess; may present as violaceous, gray, or dark brown patches; follicular pattern more common; post-inflammatory pigmentary changes (both hyper- and hypopigmentation) significant; keloid risk at excoriation sites

pregnancy_considerations: Topical corticosteroids generally safe (prefer low to mid potency; avoid potent steroids over large areas). Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, pimecrolimus) considered safe (Category C, no teratogenicity in animal studies). Avoid systemic corticosteroids if possible; cyclosporine and methotrexate generally avoided. Dupilumab and tralokinumab considered safe. Phototherapy generally safe.

name: Seborrheic Dermatitis
description: A chronic, relapsing inflammatory skin condition affecting sebum-rich areas of the body. Characterized by erythema and greasy, yellowish scaling. Associated with Malassezia yeast overgrowth, immune response, and genetic factors. Common in infants (cradle cap) and adults.
symptoms: Erythematous patches with greasy, yellowish scales
Pruritus (typically mild to moderate)
Distribution: scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, glabella, chest, intertriginous areas
Dandruff (mild scalp form)
May worsen in winter and with stress
triggers: Malassezia yeast overgrowth and immune response
Neurological conditions (Parkinson's, stroke, spinal cord injury)
red_flags: Severe, treatment-resistant seborrheic dermatitis in young adults (consider HIV testing)
Sudden onset with systemic symptoms
Widespread erythroderma
Signs of secondary bacterial infection

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Erythema and scaling readily visible; post-inflammatory erythema may persist
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Erythema may be subtle; scaling and hypopigmentation may be more noticeable
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Erythema difficult to appreciate; may present with violaceous or dark brown patches; hypopigmentation and post-inflammatory pigment changes common; scalp involvement may lead to hair breakage

pregnancy_considerations: Topical antifungals (ketoconazole, ciclopirox) and low-potency corticosteroids generally safe. Selenium sulfide and zinc pyrithione shampoos safe. Avoid systemic antifungals.

name: Psoriasis
description: A chronic, immune-mediated inflammatory disease characterized by well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery scale. Driven by Th1/Th17 immune pathways with TNF-alpha, IL-17, and IL-23 cytokines. Affects 2–3% of the population; can involve skin, nails, and joints (psoriatic arthritis).
symptoms: Well-demarcated erythematous plaques with silvery-white scale
Pruritus (variable; often mild but can be severe)
Burning and stinging sensations
Nail changes: pitting, onycholysis, subungual hyperkeratosis, oil spots
Scalp involvement with thick, adherent scale
Inverse psoriasis: smooth, red patches in intertriginous areas
Koebner phenomenon: lesions develop at sites of trauma
Auspitz sign: pinpoint bleeding when scale removed
triggers: Medications (beta-blockers, lithium, antimalarials, NSAIDs, systemic corticosteroid withdrawal)
red_flags: Erythrodermic psoriasis: generalized erythema and scaling — dermatologic emergency
Generalized pustular psoriasis (von Zumbusch): widespread pustules with fever and systemic toxicity — EMERGENCY
Joint pain, swelling, or morning stiffness (possible psoriatic arthritis)
Signs of infection in plaques

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Classic salmon-pink plaques with silvery scale; erythema readily visible
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Plaques may appear violaceous or dark red; scale may have grayish tint
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Plaques often violaceous, dark brown, or gray; scale may be less visible; hypopigmentation common in resolving plaques; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation may persist; scalp psoriasis may cause hair breakage and alopecia; diagnostic delay common due to atypical presentation

pregnancy_considerations: Topical corticosteroids and calcipotriene generally safe. Avoid systemic therapies (methotrexate absolutely contraindicated, cyclosporine, acitretin). Biologics (TNF inhibitors, IL-17/23 inhibitors) have pregnancy registry data; most considered relatively safe but discuss risks/benefits. Phototherapy (NB-UVB) safe. Psoriasis may improve, worsen, or remain unchanged during pregnancy.

name: Rosacea
description: A chronic inflammatory skin condition primarily affecting the central face, characterized by erythema, telangiectasias, papules, and pustules. Triggers include heat, alcohol, spicy foods, and emotional stress. Subtypes include erythematotelangiectatic, papulopustular, phymatous, and ocular rosacea.
symptoms: Persistent facial erythema, especially central face (cheeks, nose, chin, forehead)
Telangiectasias (visible blood vessels)
Papules and pustules (papulopustular subtype)
Flushing and burning sensations
Rhinophyma (phymatous subtype — bulbous nose enlargement)
Ocular symptoms: dry eyes, blepharitis, conjunctivitis, keratitis
Skin sensitivity and stinging
red_flags: Ocular involvement with vision changes (urgent ophthalmology referral)
Severe rhinophyma causing functional impairment
Rapid progression or atypical features (exclude lupus, dermatomyositis)
Granulomatous rosacea with non-inflammatory papules

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Erythema and telangiectasias readily visible; flushing easily observed; most common in fair-skinned individuals
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Erythema may be less obvious; may present with persistent warmth, papules, pustules, or dyschromia
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Rosacea underdiagnosed and undertreated in darker skin; may present with persistent warmth, stinging, papules, pustules, or post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation rather than visible erythema; telangiectasias harder to visualize; ocular rosacea may be missed

pregnancy_considerations: Avoid oral tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline) and isotretinoin. Topical metronidazole, azelaic acid, and ivermectin generally safe. Oral erythromycin or cephalexin if systemic antibiotics needed. Laser and light therapies generally safe but may be deferred.

name: Melasma
description: An acquired hyperpigmentation disorder characterized by symmetric, brown to gray-brown patches on sun-exposed areas, particularly the face. Driven by ultraviolet radiation, hormonal factors (estrogen, progesterone), and genetic predisposition. More common in women and individuals with darker skin phototypes.
symptoms: Symmetric brown to gray-brown macules and patches
Distribution: malar cheeks, forehead, upper lip, chin, nose (centrofacial pattern)
May also affect mandibular area or arms
Worsens with sun exposure
Often persistent and recurrent
triggers: Hormonal factors: pregnancy, oral contraceptives, hormone replacement therapy
Visible light and infrared radiation (in darker skin)
red_flags: Asymmetric pigmentation (possible other diagnosis)
Irregular borders with color variation (exclude melanoma)
Pigmentation on non-sun-exposed areas (possible medication-induced or systemic cause)
Rapid onset with systemic symptoms

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Less common; patches appear light to medium brown; sun exposure still triggers
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Common; brown to dark brown patches; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation may coexist
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Very common; brown to gray-brown or bluish patches (dermal melasma); more likely to have dermal component resistant to topical therapy; challenging to treat; higher risk of post-procedure hyperpigmentation after aggressive treatments

pregnancy_considerations: Melasma very common in pregnancy (chloasma gravidarum, 'mask of pregnancy'). Hydroquinone generally avoided in pregnancy and breastfeeding. Safe options: azelaic acid, vitamin C, niacinamide, sun protection. Most cases improve postpartum but may persist.

name: Post-Inflammatory Hyperpigmentation
description: A common acquired hyperpigmentation resulting from inflammation or injury to the skin. Caused by excess melanin deposition following inflammatory conditions (acne, eczema, psoriasis), trauma, burns, or cosmetic procedures. More pronounced and longer-lasting in darker skin phototypes.
symptoms: Brown to dark brown macules or patches at sites of prior inflammation or injury
Follows the distribution of the preceding inflammatory condition
No elevation or textural change (flat)
May persist for months to years
More prominent in sun-exposed areas
triggers: Inflammatory skin conditions (acne, eczema, psoriasis, lichen planus)
Cosmetic procedures (chemical peels, laser, microneedling) — especially in darker skin
red_flags: Pigmentation with raised, irregular borders (possible early melanoma)
Pigmentation in a changing lesion (ABCDE criteria)
Pigmentation with persistent erythema or induration
Pigmentation not improving after 12–24 months of treatment and sun protection

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Post-inflammatory erythema (PIE) more common than PIH; erythematous macules predominate
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Both PIE and PIH common; PIH tends to be medium to dark brown
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: PIH is predominant concern; hyperpigmentation often deep brown to black; persists longer; high risk of worsening with aggressive treatments; requires gentle, gradual approach; sun protection paramount

pregnancy_considerations: Focus on prevention by treating underlying inflammatory conditions gently. Safe lightening agents: azelaic acid, vitamin C, niacinamide. Avoid hydroquinone and high-strength retinoids. Sun protection essential.

name: Contact Dermatitis
description: An inflammatory skin reaction caused by direct contact with an irritant (irritant contact dermatitis, 80% of cases) or allergen (allergic contact dermatitis, delayed type IV hypersensitivity). Presents with erythema, pruritus, vesicles, and edema at the site of contact.
symptoms: Erythema, pruritus, and burning at site of contact
Vesicles and bullae in acute reactions
Edema and induration
Oozing and crusting
Lichenification in chronic cases
Well-demarcated distribution corresponding to contact area
Common allergens: nickel, fragrances, preservatives, rubber, topical antibiotics (neomycin), hair dyes (PPD)
triggers: Irritants: soaps, detergents, solvents, acids, alkalis, frequent hand washing
Allergens: nickel, fragrances, preservatives (MI/MCI, formaldehyde), rubber/latex, topical antibiotics, hair dyes, plants (poison ivy, poison oak)
red_flags: Severe facial or periorbital edema (possible airway compromise)
Widespread vesicular eruption after plant contact
Secondary bacterial infection with honey-colored crusting
Systemic symptoms (fever, malaise) suggesting severe allergic reaction
Generalized erythroderma

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Erythema and edema readily visible; vesicles prominent; post-inflammatory erythema common
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Erythema may be subtle; may present with gray, violaceous, or dark brown coloration; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation common
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Erythema difficult to appreciate; may present with dark brown, gray, or violaceous patches; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation significant and persistent; vesicles may be less visible; lichenified plaques may appear darker

pregnancy_considerations: Topical corticosteroids (low to mid potency) and emollients safe. Avoid systemic corticosteroids if possible; if severe, short course of prednisone may be used. Patch testing generally safe but may be deferred.

name: Keratosis Pilaris
description: A common, benign skin condition characterized by follicular hyperkeratosis, resulting in small, rough papules on the extensor surfaces of the upper arms, thighs, and cheeks. Often described as 'chicken skin.' Associated with atopic diathesis and dry skin. Affects up to 50–80% of adolescents and 40% of adults.
symptoms: Small, rough, follicular papules (1–2 mm)
Skin-colored to slightly erythematous or hyperpigmented
Distribution: upper arms (most common), thighs, buttocks, cheeks
Sandpaper-like texture
Worse in winter and low humidity
Often asymptomatic but may have mild pruritus
red_flags: Inflammatory, painful lesions (possible bacterial folliculitis)
Sudden onset in adulthood without prior history
Widespread or atypical distribution
Significant erythema, pustules, or scarring

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Papules may appear erythematous; post-inflammatory erythema mild
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Papules may be skin-colored or hyperpigmented; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation around follicles
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Hyperpigmented papules and surrounding post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation more prominent; follicular prominence may be more noticeable; treatments must avoid aggravating PIH

pregnancy_considerations: Generally harmless in pregnancy. Topical lactic acid, urea, and low-strength salicylic acid considered safe. Avoid high-strength retinoids. Emollients and gentle exfoliation safe.

name: Actinic Keratosis
description: A premalignant skin lesion caused by cumulative ultraviolet radiation exposure. Characterized by rough, scaly patches on sun-exposed areas. Considered an early form of squamous cell carcinoma (SCC) in situ. Risk of progression to invasive SCC is approximately 0.1–10% per lesion per year.
symptoms: Rough, dry, scaly patches or papules
Skin-colored to reddish-brown
Better felt than seen (sandpaper-like texture)
Distribution: face, scalp (bald areas), ears, neck, dorsal hands, forearms
May be tender or pruritic
Often multiple lesions (field cancerization)
triggers: Fair skin (Fitzpatrick I–II) and blue eyes
Geographic location (closer to equator, higher altitude)
red_flags: Induration, nodularity, or ulceration (possible progression to SCC)
Rapid growth or change in appearance
Bleeding or persistent crusting
Tender, erythematous nodule under scale
Multiple rapidly developing lesions in immunosuppressed patients

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Highest risk; lesions easily visible; sun damage evident; regular screening essential
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Moderate risk; lesions may be less visible; may present with hypopigmentation or subtle texture change
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Lower risk but not zero; lesions may be pigmented or dark brown; often underdiagnosed; acral and nail unit lesions more concerning; regular skin checks still important

pregnancy_considerations: Topical treatments (5-fluorouracil, imiquimod, diclofenac) generally avoided or used with caution. Cryotherapy and curettage generally safe. Photodynamic therapy typically deferred. Prevention with sun protection essential.

name: Basal Cell Carcinoma
description: The most common form of skin cancer, arising from basaloid follicular cells. Slow-growing but locally invasive. Rarely metastasizes but can cause significant local tissue destruction. Strongly associated with cumulative UV exposure, especially in fair-skinned individuals.
symptoms: Pearly papule or nodule with telangiectasias
Rolled, translucent border
Central ulceration (rodent ulcer)
Flat, scar-like or hypopigmented plaque (morpheaform subtype)
Shiny, skin-colored or pink papule (nodular subtype)
Superficial erythematous scaly patch (superficial subtype)
Distribution: face (nose most common), scalp, ears, neck, dorsal hands
triggers: Intermittent intense sun exposure and sunburns
Fair skin (Fitzpatrick I–II), blonde/red hair, blue/green eyes
Gorlin syndrome (nevoid basal cell carcinoma syndrome)
red_flags: Rapid growth or change in appearance
Recurrence after prior treatment
Perineural or periocular involvement
Large size (>2 cm) or deep invasion
Immunosuppressed patient with multiple lesions

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Highest risk; lesions typically pearly, pink, or skin-colored; early detection common
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Moderate risk; pigmented BCC may mimic melanoma; amelanotic or pink lesions still common
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Lower incidence but often diagnosed at advanced stage; pigmented BCC more common; may present as dark brown, blue, or black nodules; acral, nail unit, and mucosal lesions require heightened suspicion; regular skin checks essential despite lower risk

pregnancy_considerations: Surgical excision, Mohs micrographic surgery, and curettage/electrodessication generally safe. Radiation therapy avoided. Topical therapies (5-FU, imiquimod) generally avoided. Management should not be delayed due to pregnancy.

name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma
description: The second most common skin cancer, arising from keratinocytes. More aggressive than basal cell carcinoma with higher metastatic potential, especially when arising on lips, ears, or in immunosuppressed patients. Associated with cumulative UV exposure, HPV infection, and immunosuppression.
symptoms: Firm, hyperkeratotic papule, plaque, or nodule
Erythematous or skin-colored; may be pigmented
Central ulceration or crusting
Bleeding with minor trauma
Indurated, infiltrated base
Distribution: sun-exposed areas (scalp, face, ears, lips, dorsal hands, forearms)
May arise in scars, chronic ulcers, or areas of radiation dermatitis
triggers: Immunosuppression (organ transplant recipients have 65–250x increased risk)
Chronic scars and non-healing wounds (Marjolin ulcer)
Human papillomavirus (HPV) infection (anogenital and periungual SCC)
red_flags: Lesion >2 cm in diameter
Rapid growth or change
Recurrence after treatment
Perineural invasion symptoms (numbness, tingling, pain)
Location on lip, ear, or anogenital area (higher metastatic risk)
Immunosuppressed patient with new or changing lesion
Non-healing ulcer or lesion in scar/radiation field

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Highest risk; lesions typically erythematous, scaly, or crusted; early detection common
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Moderate risk; may present with hyperpigmentation or as dark, firm nodules
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Lower incidence but higher mortality due to delayed diagnosis; may present as hyperpigmented nodules, non-healing ulcers, or verrucous growths; acral, nail unit, and mucosal SCC more common; HPV-related and chronic scar-related SCC important; regular screening critical

pregnancy_considerations: Surgical excision and Mohs surgery generally safe and should not be delayed. Radiation and systemic chemotherapy avoided if possible. Topical treatments generally avoided. Early definitive treatment preferred.

name: Melanoma
description: A malignant tumor arising from melanocytes. The most aggressive and life-threatening form of skin cancer. Risk factors include UV exposure (especially intermittent intense exposure and blistering sunburns), fair skin, numerous moles, family history, and genetic mutations (CDKN2A). Early detection is critical for survival.
symptoms: Asymmetrical pigmented lesion with irregular borders
Color variation (black, brown, red, white, blue) within single lesion
Diameter >6 mm (but can be smaller)
Evolving: change in size, shape, color, elevation, or symptoms (itching, bleeding)
May arise de novo or from pre-existing nevus
Amelanotic (pink/red) variants occur
Nodular melanoma: rapidly growing, uniform dark nodule
Acral lentiginous melanoma: palms, soles, subungual
triggers: Intermittent intense UV exposure and blistering sunburns (especially in childhood)
Fair skin (Fitzpatrick I–II), red/blonde hair, blue/green eyes
Numerous common nevi (>50) and atypical nevi
Previous melanoma or non-melanoma skin cancer
Geographic location (Australia, New Zealand, Southern USA)
red_flags: ANY lesion meeting ABCDE criteria — urgent dermatology evaluation
New pigmented lesion in adult over age 30
Rapidly growing nodule (possible nodular melanoma)
Subungual pigmented streak with Hutchinson sign (pigment extending to nail fold)
Bleeding, ulceration, or persistent crusting of pigmented lesion
Change in pre-existing mole
Lymphadenopathy or systemic symptoms in patient with known melanoma

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Highest risk; lesions typically dark brown to black with color variation; sun damage and numerous nevi common
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Moderate risk; may present with atypical features; amelanotic melanoma possible
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Lower incidence but higher mortality due to delayed diagnosis; acral lentiginous melanoma more common; may present as dark brown, black, or blue-black lesions; subungual melanoma often misdiagnosed as fungal infection; lack of sun damage does not rule out melanoma; heightened suspicion for new or changing pigmented lesions on palms, soles, nails, and mucosa

pregnancy_considerations: Melanoma is one of the most common cancers in pregnancy. Pregnancy does not worsen prognosis but may delay diagnosis. Wide local excision and sentinel lymph node biopsy should not be delayed. Immunotherapy and targeted therapy generally avoided; discuss risks/benefits. Radiation avoided.

name: Perioral Dermatitis
description: An inflammatory facial dermatitis characterized by small papules and pustules around the mouth, nose, and eyes. Often triggered by topical corticosteroid use on the face, heavy moisturizers, and fluorinated toothpaste. More common in women aged 16–45.
symptoms: Small erythematous papules and pustules (1–2 mm)
Distribution: perioral, perinasal, and periocular areas
Sparing of the immediate vermilion border (characteristic)
Burning and tight sensation
Mild pruritus
Background erythema and scaling
triggers: Topical corticosteroid use on face (especially potent steroids)
Heavy facial moisturizers and occlusive cosmetics
red_flags: Severe or rapidly worsening eruption
Signs of secondary bacterial infection
No improvement after 8–12 weeks of appropriate treatment
Granulomatous perioral dermatitis (non-itchy, skin-colored papules in darker skin)

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Erythema and papules readily visible; post-inflammatory erythema common
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Papules may be skin-colored or hyperpigmented; erythema less obvious
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Granulomatous variant more common; skin-colored or hyperpigmented papules; erythema difficult to appreciate; may be misdiagnosed as acne; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation common

pregnancy_considerations: Topical metronidazole, azelaic acid, and erythromycin generally safe. Oral tetracyclines avoided; oral erythromycin if systemic treatment needed. Avoid topical corticosteroids. Gentle skincare essential.

name: Folliculitis
description: An inflammatory condition of the hair follicle caused by bacterial infection (Staphylococcus aureus most common), fungal infection (Malassezia), mechanical irritation, or chemical exposure. Presents as small pustules centered on hair follicles.
symptoms: Small pustules with central hair (follicular papules and pustules)
Erythema and mild tenderness around lesions
Pruritus or burning
Distribution: beard area, scalp, buttocks, thighs, axillae, any hair-bearing area
May be superficial or deep (furuncles, carbuncles)
red_flags: Deep, painful nodules (possible furuncle or carbuncle)
Fever and systemic symptoms (possible cellulitis or abscess)
Widespread or recurrent folliculitis (possible immunosuppression or MRSA colonization)
Scarring or permanent hair loss (possible scarring alopecia)
Non-healing lesions in immunocompromised patients

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Pustules and erythema readily visible; post-inflammatory erythema common
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation common after resolution
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation significant and persistent; central hair may be more visible in pustules; keloid risk at sites of deep folliculitis; pseudofolliculitis barbae frequently coexists

pregnancy_considerations: Topical benzoyl peroxide, clindamycin, and mupirocin generally safe. Oral antibiotics (cephalexin, dicloxacillin) safe if needed. Avoid oral tetracyclines and fluoroquinolones. Warm compresses and hygiene measures safe.

name: Tinea Versicolor
description: A superficial fungal infection caused by Malassezia species (lipophilic yeast), characterized by discolored macules and patches on the trunk and proximal extremities. Not contagious. More common in warm, humid climates and in adolescents/young adults.
symptoms: Hypopigmented, hyperpigmented, or pink/tan macules and patches
Fine scaling, especially with scraping
Distribution: chest, back, upper arms, neck (seborrheic areas)
Asymptomatic or mild pruritus
Worse with sun exposure (hypopigmented lesions become more apparent)
May coalesce into large patches
triggers: Malassezia yeast overgrowth (lipophilic yeast normal skin flora)
red_flags: Lesions not responding to antifungal treatment (possible other diagnosis)
Widespread or persistent infection in immunocompromised patient
Atypical distribution or appearance

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Hypopigmented or pink/tan patches; less visible on fair skin
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Hyperpigmented or hypopigmented patches; more noticeable
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Hypopigmented patches highly visible and cosmetically concerning; often misdiagnosed as vitiligo; hyperpigmented variants also occur; post-inflammatory pigmentary changes may persist for months after treatment; recurrence common

pregnancy_considerations: Topical antifungals (selenium sulfide, ketoconazole shampoo, zinc pyrithione) generally safe. Oral antifungals (itraconazole, fluconazole) generally avoided. Topical terbinafine less effective for tinea versicolor.

name: Cold Sores / HSV-1
description: A recurrent viral infection caused by herpes simplex virus type 1 (HSV-1), characterized by grouped vesicles on an erythematous base, typically around the lips and perioral area. Highly contagious. After primary infection, HSV establishes latency in trigeminal ganglia and reactivates periodically.
symptoms: Prodrome: tingling, burning, itching, or pain at site 12–24 hours before eruption
Grouped vesicles on erythematous base
Vesicles rupture forming painful ulcers with honey-colored crusting
Distribution: lips (vermilion border), perioral skin, nose
Regional lymphadenopathy
May have systemic symptoms (fever, malaise) with primary infection
Recurrent episodes at same site
triggers: Viral reactivation from latency in trigeminal ganglia
Fever and illness (hence 'cold sore' or 'fever blister')
red_flags: Eczema herpeticum: widespread HSV infection in patient with eczema — EMERGENCY
Ocular involvement: keratitis, blepharitis — urgent ophthalmology referral
Herpetic whitlow: painful vesicles on fingers
Severe, persistent, or atypical lesions in immunocompromised patients
Encephalitis symptoms (headache, fever, altered mental status) — EMERGENCY

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Erythema and vesicles readily visible; crusting prominent; post-inflammatory erythema common
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Erythema may be subtle; vesicles and crusting visible; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation common
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Erythema difficult to appreciate; may present with dark crusting or grayish patches; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation significant; perioral PIH can be persistent; eczema herpeticum may have atypical presentation

pregnancy_considerations: Primary HSV infection during pregnancy is concerning (risk of neonatal herpes). Acyclovir, valacyclovir considered safe in pregnancy. Suppressive therapy near delivery if recurrent genital HSV. Avoid kissing newborn if active cold sore.

name: Razor Bumps / Pseudofolliculitis Barbae
description: An inflammatory condition caused by shaved hairs re-entering the skin (transfollicular penetration) or growing parallel to the skin surface (extrafollicular penetration), triggering a foreign body inflammatory response. Very common in individuals with curly hair, particularly African descent. Also called 'shave bumps' or 'razor bumps.'
symptoms: Follicular papules and pustules in shaved areas
Pruritus and tenderness
Hair may be visible within or beneath the papule
Distribution: beard area, neck (shaved areas), scalp (if shaved), axillae, groin
May lead to post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and keloid formation
Can cause pseudocysts and scarring in chronic cases
triggers: Shaving, especially close shaving and against the grain
Curly or coarse hair (more likely to re-enter skin)
Wearing tight clothing over shaved areas
red_flags: Severe scarring or keloid formation
Large inflammatory nodules or pseudocysts
Secondary bacterial infection with honey-colored crusting
Significant psychological distress or social impact
Not improving with behavioral modifications after 4–6 weeks

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Papules and pustules visible; post-inflammatory erythema common; less common in straight hair
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Common; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation prominent
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Very common and significant concern; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation and keloid formation major issues; pseudofolliculitis barbae can cause disfiguring keloids (acne keloidalis nuchae on posterior neck); requires culturally sensitive approach; hair texture and grooming practices must be considered

pregnancy_considerations: Behavioral modifications (growing beard, proper shaving technique) preferred. Topical clindamycin or benzoyl peroxide safe. Avoid depilatory creams (chemical irritation risk). Electrolysis and laser hair removal generally safe but may be deferred.

name: Stretch Marks
description: Linear, atrophic scars caused by rapid stretching of the skin beyond its elastic capacity, resulting in dermal collagen and elastin disruption. Initially erythematous or violaceous (striae rubrae), then evolve to hypopigmented, atrophic scars (striae albae). Common during puberty, pregnancy, rapid weight gain/loss, and corticosteroid use.
symptoms: Linear, band-like streaks parallel to skin tension lines
Striae rubrae: early, erythematous to violaceous, slightly raised
Striae albae: mature, hypopigmented, atrophic, wrinkled
Distribution: abdomen (especially pregnancy), breasts, thighs, hips, buttocks, upper arms, lower back
Initially may be pruritic
Asymptomatic once mature
triggers: Rapid skin stretching (growth spurts, pregnancy, weight gain/loss)
Cushing syndrome and chronic corticosteroid therapy
Breast augmentation or rapid muscle growth
red_flags: Widespread striae with signs of Cushing syndrome (central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump, hypertension)
Striae with easy bruising and skin fragility
Atypical distribution (shoulders, axillae, face) suggesting Cushing or anabolic steroid use
Purple, wide striae in non-obese patient (possible Cushing)

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Striae rubrae: pink to red; striae albae: pearly white; highly visible on fair skin
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Striae rubrae: violaceous to dark red; striae albae: lighter than surrounding skin; still visible
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Striae rubrae: dark purple, brown, or black; striae albae: hypopigmented, often shiny and silvery; may be less visible than in fair skin; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation may surround early lesions; treatment response to lasers more challenging due to risk of pigmentary changes

pregnancy_considerations: Very common in pregnancy (striae gravidarum). Topical treatments (tretinoin) generally avoided; some use cautiously after first trimester. Moisturizers and oils may help prevent but evidence limited. Striae usually improve postpartum but may not fully resolve. Laser treatments deferred until after pregnancy and breastfeeding.

name: Scarring
description: A permanent fibrous tissue replacement of normal skin following injury, inflammation, or surgery. Results from abnormal wound healing with excessive or deficient collagen deposition. Types include atrophic (depressed), hypertrophic (raised, within wound boundaries), and keloid (raised, beyond wound boundaries) scars.
symptoms: Atrophic scars: depressed, pitted, or ice-pick lesions (common in acne)
Hypertrophic scars: raised, erythematous, confined to original wound boundaries
Keloid scars: raised, firm, extending beyond original wound boundaries
Pruritus and pain (especially hypertrophic and keloid scars)
Contractures (in burn scars)
Texture and color changes
triggers: Deep skin injury (acne cysts, burns, surgery, trauma)
Inflammatory skin conditions (acne, chickenpox, hidradenitis suppurativa)
Genetic predisposition (keloid formation more common in Fitzpatrick V–VI)
red_flags: Rapidly enlarging keloid with pain or ulceration (rarely, keloid sarcoma)
Contractures limiting function (especially over joints)
Scarring with persistent erythema and elevation (possible keloid or hypertrophic scar requiring treatment)
Significant psychological distress or functional impairment

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Atrophic acne scarring common; hypertrophic scars less common; keloids rare; post-inflammatory erythema prominent in healing
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Mixed scarring types; keloid risk moderate; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation may surround scars
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Keloid formation significantly more common and severe; even minor trauma, piercings, or acne can cause large keloids; central chest, shoulders, back, and earlobes high-risk sites; atrophic acne scarring also common; treatment of keloids more challenging; higher recurrence after treatment; hypertrophic scarring more common

pregnancy_considerations: Existing keloids may enlarge during pregnancy due to hormonal changes. Treatment of scars generally safe (silicone gel, compression, intralesional corticosteroids). Laser treatments and retinoids often deferred. Surgical revision of keloids generally avoided during pregnancy.

name: Dark Circles
description: Periorbital hyperpigmentation and shadowing causing dark discoloration under the lower eyelids. Multifactorial etiology including genetics, melanin deposition, vascular congestion, skin thinning, and tear trough deformity. Very common and often a cosmetic concern.
symptoms: Brown, blue, or purple discoloration under eyes
Worse with fatigue and sleep deprivation
May have associated puffiness or bags
Tear trough depression creating shadow effect
Visible vascularity (blue/purple hue)
Often bilateral and symmetric
triggers: Genetics and family history (most common cause)
Aging and skin thinning (dermal collagen loss makes vessels more visible)
red_flags: Unilateral darkening (possible orbital mass or vascular lesion)
Sudden onset with other systemic symptoms
Dark circles with severe fatigue and pallor (possible anemia)
Periorbital swelling with pain or vision changes (urgent evaluation needed)

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Vascular component (blue/purple) often prominent; shadow effect from thin skin; melanin component less common
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Mixed vascular and pigmentary components; brown discoloration common
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Melanin-driven hyperpigmentation predominant; genetic/familial pigmentation very common; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation from eczema or allergies significant; vascular component less visible; tear trough deformity still contributes to shadowing; treatments must address hyperpigmentation cautiously

pregnancy_considerations: Dark circles may worsen during pregnancy due to hormonal changes, fluid retention, and sleep disturbances. Safe options: cold compresses, adequate sleep, sun protection, topical vitamin C, caffeine eye creams. Avoid retinoids and hydroquinone. Most improve postpartum.

name: Xerosis / Dry Skin
description: A common condition characterized by decreased skin moisture and impaired skin barrier function, resulting in rough, scaly, tight, and sometimes pruritic skin. Can be physiologic (aging, winter) or pathologic (eczema, ichthyosis, hypothyroidism, systemic disease). Very common, especially in older adults.
symptoms: Rough, scaly skin texture
Tightness, especially after bathing
Mild to moderate pruritus
Fine cracks or fissures (severe cases)
Ashy or grayish appearance in darker skin
Flaking and peeling
Distribution: extensor lower legs (most common), arms, hands, trunk
triggers: Low environmental humidity (winter, heated indoor air)
red_flags: Severe xerosis with systemic symptoms (possible hypothyroidism, diabetes, kidney disease)
Generalized severe pruritus without rash (possible cholestasis, hematologic malignancy)
Xerosis with failure to thrive in infants (possible ichthyosis or metabolic disorder)
Severe fissuring with bleeding or infection
Xerosis unresponsive to maximal emollient therapy

fitzpatrick_considerations.I_II: Skin appears flaky and rough; erythema may be present; fine white scale
fitzpatrick_considerations.III_IV: Skin may appear dull and ashy; scale less visible; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation at excoriation sites
fitzpatrick_considerations.V_VI: Prominent 'ashy' appearance — gray, whitish, or silvery scale on dark skin; often undertreated due to cultural perceptions; may be dismissed as 'just dry skin' when significant; post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation at scratch marks; higher risk of eczema and dermatitis in context of xerosis

pregnancy_considerations: Very common in pregnancy due to hormonal changes and increased skin stretching. Emollients, gentle cleansers, and humidifiers safe. Avoid hot baths. Urea and lactic acid moisturizers generally safe. Address underlying conditions (thyroid, dehydration).
